content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement · · Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662           -322-5660              322-5901           322-6441              322-6444


September 11, 1981


Anne Krummenacher
Manatt, Phelps, Rothenberg & Tunney
1888 Century Park East, 21st Floor
Los Angeles,CA    90067

Dear Ms. Krummenacher:

This is in response to your letter of August 6, 1981, in which you
asked for clarification of what must be deemed "compensation" for
applying the Compensation Test contained in 2 California Administrative
Code Section 18239(e)(1).

The specific definitions you presented include:

     (1)  Only time spent in direct communication as defined in 2
          California Administrative Code Section 18239(b) is includ-
          ed as compensation.

     (2)  Payments received by the firm that are not compensation
          paid to an employee (i.e., firm overhead) are not
          included.

     (3)  Only that portion of payments to the firm that represents
          the partners pro-rata share need be allocated to the indi-
          vidual partner as compensation.

Items (1) and (2) are correct, however, the client making such payments
to the firm may incur a filing obligation as one spending $2500 in a
calendar quarter based on total payments to influence legislature or
administrative action.

Anne Krummenacher
September 11, 1981
Page 2


Item (3) is essentially correct, but as a further point of
clarification, previous staff advice regarding compensation to members
of a law firm has included the following guidelines. If a firm member
shares in the profits of the partnership and draws money in lieu of
salary against earnings, the member would calculate what his or her
share of the profit form the clients payments to the firm would be
based on part earnings to calculate compensation.

The above explanations are for the purpose of determining whether a
person qualifies as a lobbyist pursuant to the Compensation Test in 2
California Administrative Code Section 18239(e)[1]. A person may also
qualify as a lobbyist pursuant to the Time Test in subsection(e)(2).

If I can be of nay further assistance, please do not hesitate to con-
tact me.

Yours Very Truly,

[signature]

Lynn Montgomery
Political Reform Consultant

MANATT, PHELPS, ROTHENBERG & TUNNEY
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS

ATTORNEYS AT LAW
1888 CENTURY PARK EAST
TWENTY-FIRST FLOOR
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90067

TELEPHONE (213) 556-1800

CABLE: MANATOP LSA
TWO BIG 490-5744

OF COUNSEL
RODNEY C. HILL†
BRUCE J. ZEPPA†

WASHINGTON OFFICE
200 NEW HAMPSHIRE AVE., N.W.
SUITE 200
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20036
TELEPHONE (202) 833-4300

SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE
100 BUSH STREET
SUITE 2344
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94104
TELEPHONE (415) 981-7640

August 6, 1981

Ms. Lynn Montgomery
Political Reform Consultant
Fair Political Practices Commission
700 "K" Street, 2nd Floor
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ms. Montgomery:

The purpose of this letter is to confirm our conversations of July 27, 1981 and August 4, 1981. As you know, this firm was originally retained by a client for representation before the California Department of Corporations on a securities matter. Pursuant to that representation, and incidental thereto, it will be necessary for members of this firm to be in "direct communication" with members of the California Legislature. Specifically, the following members of the firm will be communicating with legislators:

     Willie R. Barnes, partner
     Michael Kantor, partner
     Peter D. Kelly, associate
     Anne H. Krummenacher, associate

MANATT, PHELPS, ROTHENBERG & TUNNEY


Ms. Lynn Montgomery
August 6, 1981
Page Two (2)


     Based on our review of (1) the applicable statutory pro-
visions (2) the Commission's regulations and (3) your comments,
we do not expect that registration will be required for any of
the named individuals. This conclusion is based on our
expectation that "compensation" will not exceed $1,000, based
on the parameters for the computation of compensation outlined
by you as follows:

     1. Only time spent in "direct communication" with
        legislators (or their staffs) is includable.

     2. Overhead of the firm may be excluded from
        "compensation."

     3. Only that portion of compensation paid to the
        firm that represents the partner's pro rata
        share of partnership remuneration from this
        client need be allocated to him as an
        individual.

     Should "compensation" as defined above exceed $1,000 the
individuals involved will, of course, immediately register with
the Commission.

     Thank you again for your clarification and assistance.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
AHK:shs                                 Anne H. Krummenacher
                                        of Manatt, Phelps,
                                        Rothenberg & Tunney

sections.question: What must be deemed "compensation" for applying the Compensation Test contained in 2 California Administrative Code Section 18239(e)(1)? Specifically: (1) Only time spent in direct communication as defined in 2 California Administrative Code Section 18239(b) is included as compensation. (2) Payments received by the firm that are not compensation paid to an employee (i.e., firm overhead) are not included. (3) Only that portion of payments to the firm that represents the partners pro-rata share need be allocated to the individual partner as compensation.
sections.conclusion: Items (1) and (2) are correct, however, the client making such payments to the firm may incur a filing obligation as one spending $2500 in a calendar quarter based on total payments to influence legislative or administrative action. Item (3) is essentially correct, but as a further point of clarification, previous staff advice regarding compensation to members of a law firm has included the following guidelines. If a firm member shares in the profits of the partnership and draws money in lieu of salary against earnings, the member would calculate what his or her share of the profit form the clients payments to the firm would be based on part earnings to calculate compensation.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks for clarification on what constitutes "compensation" under the lobbyist compensation test, specifically regarding time spent in direct communication, firm overhead exclusions, and pro-rata allocation of partnership compensation.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that only direct communication time and employee compensation (excluding overhead) count, and confirms pro-rata allocation for partners; however, the client may have separate filing obligations based on total expenditures exceeding $2,500 in a calendar quarter.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a dual-document response letter where the FPPC response (dated September 11, 1981) follows the incoming request (dated August 6, 1981). The question and conclusion sections are clearly identifiable in the FPPC response. Minor OCR issues present (e.g., 'Rothenberg' appears as 'Rothenber0' in header, 'nay' instead of 'any'). Document discusses lobbyist registration requirements and compensation calculations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What must be deemed "compensation" for applying the Compensation Test contained in 2 California Administrative Code Section 18239(e)(1)? Specifically: (1) Only time spent in direct communication as defined in 2 California Administrative Code Section 18239(b) is included as compensation. (2) Payments received by the firm that are not compensation paid to an employee (i.e., firm overhead) are not included. (3) Only that portion of payments to the firm that represents the partners pro-rata share need be allocated to the individual partner as compensation.

CONCLUSION: Items (1) and (2) are correct, however, the client making such payments to the firm may incur a filing obligation as one spending $2500 in a calendar quarter based on total payments to influence legislative or administrative action. Item (3) is essentially correct, but as a further point of clarification, previous staff advice regarding compensation to members of a law firm has included the following guidelines. If a firm member shares in the profits of the partnership and draws money in lieu of salary against earnings, the member would calculate what his or her share of the profit form the clients payments to the firm would be based on part earnings to calculate compensation.

embedding.summary: FPPC clarifies the definition of "compensation" for determining lobbyist status under the compensation test, confirming that direct communication time and pro-rata partnership compensation apply, while firm overhead is excluded. The advice notes that clients themselves may face registration obligations based on total expenditures regardless of individual compensation thresholds.
